coredata.dc:title: A novel ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor based on M-shaped functional DNA complexes for simultaneous detection of trace lead and mercury ions in series aquatic edible vegetables
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaoying
Xu, Mingming
Kuang, Yijing
Liu, Xiangping
Yuan, Jinhua
coredata.dc:description: Simultaneous quantification of multiple heavy metal ions (HMIs) is essential due to enhanced toxicity induced by synergistic effects. The currently available detection methods suffer from drawbacks such as costly devices, poor anti-interference, and specificity. In this work, a ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor for simultaneous detection of trace lead (Pb2+) and mercury ions (Hg2+) was developed. Here, a metal-organic framework, UiO-66-CNTs, with inherent and stable electrochemical signal was used for loading complementary strands (CSs) and internal reference. Guanine-rich and thymine-rich oligonucleotides, labelled with carbon dots (CDs), acted as aptamers (Apts) and hybridized with CSs to form M-shaped DNA complexes. Pb2+ and Hg2+ could be recognized and captured by Apts to form Pb2+-G-quadruplex and T-Hg2+-T complexes, leading to the destruction of M-shaped DNA complexes and changes in CDs’ signal. The current ratios, I CDs/I UiO-66-CNTs, were applied to quantify Pb2+ and Hg2+. Benefiting from the anti-interference ability of the ratiometric strategy and the specificity of Apts, the proposed method enabled detection Pb2+ and Hg2+ through simple instrumentation, with detection limits of 2.0 ng mL−1 and 0.5 ng mL−1. Moreover, it was applied to assess Pb2+ and Hg2+ contamination in series of aquatic edible vegetables. The experimental results were consistent with inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), demonstrating its potential in practical applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor for simultaneous detection of trace Pb2+ and Hg2+.
(A) The SEM image and (B) magnified SEM image of UiO-66-CNTs. (C) EDS characterization of UiO-66-CNTs. (D) FTIR spectra of UiO-66-CNTs (a) and UiO-66 (b).

Photographs of CDP (A) and CDH (B) under daylight (upper layer) and 365 nm UV light (low layer). (C) Zeta potential of CDs. TEM image of CDP (D) and CDH (E), the inset was HRTEM image. (F) Hydrated particle size, (G) UV–vis spectra, (H) Fluorescence emission spectra, (I) DPV curves of CDP and CDH. The dashed and solid lines in H and I represented low-concentration solution at 0.02 mg mL−1 and high-concentration solutions at 0.1 mg mL−1, respectively.

EIS of the GCE with different modifications: GCE (a), UiO-66-CNTs/GCE (b), CSTri/GCE (c), Apts/GCE (M-shaped/GCE) (d) and Hg2+&Pb2+/GCE with 10 ng mL−1 Pb2+ and Hg2+(e). The inset was the EIS of the GCE with different modifications: GCE (a′), UiO-66-CNTs/GCE (b′), UiO-66/GCE (c′).

(A-C) Comparison of series functional Apts/GCE. (A) without targets (curve a), M-shaped/GCE (curve b) and L′-shaped/GCE (curve c) with targets, (B) without targets (curve a), M-shaped/GCE (curve b) and M′-shaped/GCE (curve c) with targets, (C) without targets (curve a), M-shaped/GCE (curve b) and L-shaped/GCE (curve c) with targets. (D-F) Cross-reactivity of different electrodes. (D) without targets (curve a), CDP-PBA/GCE (curve b) with targets, (E) without targets (curve a), CDH-HBA/GCE (curve b) with targets. (F) M-shaped/GCE, without targets (curve a), with Pb2+ (curve b), with Hg2+ (curve c), with Pb2+ and Hg2+ (curve d and e). The concentrations of Pb2+ and Hg2+ were 100 ng mL−1 in curve e of Fig. 5F, and they were 10 ng mL−1 in all other figures. The inset was the corresponding bar graph of corresponding to DPV response.
Effect of (A) hybridization time, (B) hybridization temperature, (C) the incubation time of Pb2+ and Hg2+, and (D) pH of the detection buffer.

The current responses of M-shaped/GCE to Pb2+ and Hg2+ with the influence of (A) non-target cations, (B) non-target inorganics and organics. The stability of (C) UiO-66-CNTs/GCE, (D) M-shaped/GCE. (E) The reproducibility and (F) the regeneration of M-shaped/GCE.

(A) DPV curves of the developed aptasensor toward Pb2+ and Hg2+ with different concentrations (0, 1, 5, 10, 100, 1000 ng mL−1) (B) The calibration plots for simultaneous Pb2+ and Hg2+ determination (I CDP/I UiO-66-CNTs and I CDP/I UiO-66-CNTs vs. logarithm of the Pb2+ and Hg2+ concentration, respectively). The calibration plots for Pb2+ and Hg2+ determination based on individual (C) Pb2+, (D) Hg2+, respectively.
Comparison with analytical performances of typical literatures on the simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+.

head.ce:title: A novel ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor based on M-shaped functional DNA complexes for simultaneous detection of trace lead and mercury ions in series aquatic edible vegetables

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simultaneous quantification of multiple heavy metal ions (HMIs) is essential due to enhanced toxicity induced by synergistic effects. The currently available detection methods suffer from drawbacks such as costly devices, poor anti-interference, and specificity. In this work, a ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor for simultaneous detection of trace lead (Pb2+) and mercury ions (Hg2+) was developed. Here, a metal-organic framework, UiO-66-CNTs, with inherent and stable electrochemical signal was used for loading complementary strands (CSs) and internal reference. Guanine-rich and thymine-rich oligonucleotides, labelled with carbon dots (CDs), acted as aptamers (Apts) and hybridized with CSs to form M-shaped DNA complexes. Pb2+ and Hg2+ could be recognized and captured by Apts to form Pb2+-G-quadruplex and T-Hg2+-T complexes, leading to the destruction of M-shaped DNA complexes and changes in CDs’ signal. The current ratios, I CDs/I UiO-66-CNTs, were applied to quantify Pb2+ and Hg2+. Benefiting from the anti-interference ability of the ratiometric strategy and the specificity of Apts, the proposed method enabled detection Pb2+ and Hg2+ through simple instrumentation, with detection limits of 2.0 ng mL−1 and 0.5 ng mL−1. Moreover, it was applied to assess Pb2+ and Hg2+ contamination in series of aquatic edible vegetables. The experimental results were consistent with inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), demonstrating its potential in practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution is widespread in air, water bodies and soil. Increasing heavy metal pollution causes serious environmental damage and human harm [1–3]. Notably, it is often caused by several kinds of heavy metal ions (HMIs) simultaneously. When HMIs with synergistic and additive effects co-exist, more serious results are often produced [4,5]. For example, exposure to Pb2+ would cause anemia and reproductive toxicity, as well as low learning skills [6]. And the excessive intake of Hg2+ would induce the damage of many physiological organs and systems [7,8]. Worse still, more serious health risks would be posed with the concurrent presence of Pb2+ and Hg2+. Numerous studies have shown that Pb2+ and Hg2+ often co-exist and induce severe harm due to synergistic effects, such as nervous system disruption, carcinogenesis, genotoxicity, and even death [9]. Aquatic edible vegetables are susceptible to heavy metal contamination through absorption or transfer of heavy metals from water bodies, silt deposits and the atmosphere due to long-term growth in water and soil [10]. For example, Xiong et al. [11] investigated the heavy metal pollution in eight major lotus root producing areas in China and found that Pb2+ in one area and Hg2+ in two areas exceeded the standard. Thus, searching for efficient and sensitive methods to detect multiple HMIs contamination in aquatic edible vegetables is of great significance for practical applications.

To date, many methods have been developed to detect a wide variety of trace amounts of multiple HMIs. For instance, atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), as national standard methods, can achieve the accurate and sensitive quantification of multiple HMIs simultaneously. However, the need for expensive instruments, complex experimental conditions, and professional operations limits their wider application [12]. To solve the above problems, sensing methods with simple operation and flexible detection conditions appeared and developed rapidly. Currently, the traditional sensing methods realize multiple HMIs quantification mainly through combined or simultaneous detection. Combined detection does not allow for multiple targets to be tested at once and relies on repetition of the experimental procedures to achieve multi-target quantification, which is costly in terms of time and money. In comparison, simultaneous detection, which requires only one experimental procedure to complete the detection of multiple HMIs, is more efficient and economical [13]. In the simultaneous detection of multiple HMIs, optical techniques, such as ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), Surface-enhanced raman spectroscopy (SERS), colorimetry, and fluorescence are commonly used. Among them, fluorescence sensors are applied most widely since HMIs could produce fluorescence quenching or fluorescence enhancement on fluorescent substances. Nevertheless, light source fluctuations, light scattering from the sample matrix, and the changes in the microenvironment could impact the test results [14]. In comparison, electrochemical sensors show several important advantages including low cost, simplicity, high selectivity, and sensitivity. Furthermore, electrochemical sensors offer unique advantages in the simultaneous detection of HMIs due to the specific redox signals of the HMIs [15]. However, the poor reproducibility caused by the inevitable variation in parallel electrode characteristics and the complex detection environment are the main challenges for the practical application of traditional electrochemical sensors [16].

Ratiometric analysis provides a solution to the above problem. Ratiometric analysis relies on two signals for quantification and at least one signal changes linearly with the targets [17]. It can normalize electrode and environmental variables through built-in correction and improve the reproducibility and reliability of electrochemical sensors [18]. Depending on the source of the detection signals, the detection modes of the ratiometric electrochemical sensors can be further categorized into direct and indirect detection. Direct detection is performed based on the redox signals of HMIs. For example, Wang et al. [19] developed a ratiometric electrochemical sensor for the simultaneously detection of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ based on a composite of ferrocene-functionalized metal-organic framework, and thermally reduced graphene oxide (trGNO/Fc-NH2-UiO-66). The redox signals of three HMIs and the current signal of the trGNO/Fc-NH2-UiO-66 were utilized as the response and the reference signals to achieve ratiometric detection. The limits of detections (LODs) of the sensor were estimated to be 8.5 nM for Cd2+, 0.6 nM for Pb2+ and 0.8 nM for Cu2+, respectively. Moreover, the practicality of the sensor was also validated by the recovery test of target metal ions in tap water. Unfortunately, direct detection is susceptible to environmental interference, and the redox signals of HMIs often stick together and are difficult to distinguish. Liu et al. [20] realized the indirect detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+ by the ratio of the current of the electroactive substances. Aanthraquinone-2-carboxylic acid (AQ)-labeled ssDNA acted as complementary DNA, and simultaneously bound with ferrocene (Fc)-labeled aptamer for Hg2+ and methylene blue (MB)-labeled aptamer for Pb2+, and the sensing interface was fabricated with this presynthesized DNA structure. The current signal of AQ (I AQ) was used as a reference. Currents of Fc (I Fc) and MB (I MB) were used to produce ratiometric signals of I AQ/I Fc and I AQ/I MB for the detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+, with LODs of 0.69 pM and 0.093 pM, respectively. It was successfully used for soil, water, and crayfish analysis in paddy fields. Indirect detection quantifies HMIs with the help of recognition elements such as aptamers, which can effectively improve specificity and anti-interference ability of the ratiometric electrochemical sensor. Obviously, indirect detection has more advantages than direct detection, but there is only one relevant report mentioned above, and it urgently needs to be developed.

Inspired by the above research background and related works, some new attempts were carried out and a ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor for simultaneous quantitative detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ was developed. The schematic of Pb2+ and Hg2+ sensing with the proposed methods is depicted in Fig. 1. To improve the specificity of the detection, aptamers (Apts) were used as recognition elements. For Pb2+ detection, one typical strategy was dependent on the specific binding of Pb2+ to the guanine (G)-rich oligonucleotide to form the stable Pb2+-quadruplex structure [21]. As for Hg2+, it has been reported that the oligonucleotide with rich thymine (T) can selectively capture Hg2+ to form the T-Hg2+-T units [22]. Furthermore, carbon tubes-doped Zr-MOF (UiO-66-CNTs) with large specific surface area as an electrode modification material allowed more complementary chains (CSs) to be loaded. More importantly, UiO-66-CNTs itself had a specific electrochemical signal, which omitted the process of doping additional electroactive substances. Two carbon dots (CDs), CDP and CDH, were labeled with Pb2+ binding Apt (PBA) and Hg2+ binding Apt (HPA), respectively. Notably, as an intrinsic property of the CDs, the fluorescence signal was susceptible to decrease due to self-quenching with the tendency to aggregate [23,24]. In contrast, the electrochemical signal was more stable and can be further enhanced after aggregation. Therefore, the strong peak currents of CDs served as response signals. Two Apts were hybridized with three CSTri (CS1, CS2, and CS3) loaded on the UiO-66-CNTs to form M-shaped functional DNA complexes. Compared to linear DNA, the rigid M-shape structure was more stable and helped to prevent collapsing of the DNA chains. More importantly, it favored the generation of signals of CDs and thus improved detection sensitivity. When Pb2+ or Hg2+ was present, it tended to form stable and specific Pb2+-G-quadruplex or T-Hg2+-T structures and fell off from the electrode surface. As a result, the peak currents of CDs (I CDP, I CDH) decreased while that of UiO-66-CNTs (I UiO-66-CNTs) remained unchanged. Benefit from the highly specific recognition of the Apts to the HMIs and the sufficiently large separation of electrochemical potentials among CDP, CDH, and UiO-66-CNTs, the proposed aptasensor can be used for both independent and simultaneous analysis of Pb2+ and Hg2+ through the ratiometric signals of I CDP/I UiO-66-CNTs and I CDH/I UiO-66-CNTs, respectively. This work provides a simple and effective strategy for the construction of ratiometric aptasensor with high sensitivity and anti-interference capability for the simultaneous analysis of two different HMIs.

All oligonucleotides were provided by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) as shown in Table S1. Hg(NO3)2 and Pb(NO3)2 were obtained by Aladdin Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA), tris(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), 2-aminoterephthalic acid, and zirconium (IV) chloride were from Aladdin Biotech (Shanghai, China). Multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs, 2–30 nm and 0.1–50 µm in diameter and length, respectively) were from Xfnano Biomart (Nanjing, China). O-phenylenediamine (o-PD), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), glucose, citrate, urea, ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO-d6), and 6-Mercaptohexanol (MCH) were offered by Heowns (Tianjin, China). Buffers used in the experiments are as follows. 10 mM Tris-HCl was set to the washing buffer and detection buffer. Moreover, it was provided for preparation buffer (containing 10 mM TCEP), hybridization buffer (containing 2 mM MgCl2, 10 mM KCl, and 0.3 M NaCl). The pH of all buffers was 7.4.

Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) was conducted by Nova NanoSEM450 (FEI, USA). The images of Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) were performed by S-4800 SEM microscope (JEOL, Japan). Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) was performed with a Zetasizer (Malvern, England). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was obtained by the FTIR 6300 spectral system (JASCO, Japan). 1 Dimension-H nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (.

1H NMR) was recorded by AVIII-500 MHz FT NMR (Bruker, USA). Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer (ICP-MS) was from Aurora M90 (Bruker, Germany). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), cyclic Voltammetry (CV), and anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) were recorded by CHI760D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, China).

Two carbon dots (CDs), CDP and CDH, were synthesized based on Wang’s [25] and Gao’s [26] report with slight modifications. In a typical procedure for the synthesis of CDP, citric acid (1.26 g) and urea (1.08 g) were added to ultrapure water (30 mL). The mixed solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and then kept in an oven at 180 ℃ for 8 h for further reaction. The obtained CDP solution was centrifuged (10,000 r min −1, 10 min) and dialyzed for 24 h, and was then freeze-dried for further reaction after cooling down to room temperature (RT). The gained CDP was dispersed in ultrapure water for further use. Likely, CDH was synthesized as follows. o-PD (2.0 g) was dissolved in DMF (40 mL). Then the mixture was further reacted at 220 ℃ for 5 h. After the reactor was cooled down to RT automatically, the obtained solution was purified via 0.22 µm filter. After removing the solvent through a rotary evaporator, the obtained products were dispersed in ultrapure water and centrifuged to remove unreacted materials, and then freeze-dried. The gained CDH was dispersed in ultrapure water for further use.

To prepare CDP-labelled PBA (CDP-PBA), CDP and PBA were stirred at 4 ℃ for 2 h. After further centrifuging and washing, the CDP-PBA was obtained and stored at 4 ℃ for further use. CDH-HBA was obtained in the same way. Furthermore, CDP-NPA and CDH-NHA were prepared by substituting NPA and NHA for PBA and HBA as described above.
The specific ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor were fabricated as follows.

In this work, carbon tube-doped Zr-MOF (UiO-66-CNTs) was applied as an internal reference, which was synthesized according to Wang’s [27] work with a little modification. Firstly, MWCNTs were highly dispersed in DMF (50 mL) under ultrasonic for over 1 h. After uniform dispersion, 2-aminoterephthalic (90 mg) and zirconium (IV) chloride (120 mg) were added to the above solution, with ultrasonication and stirring until the solutes were completely dissolved. The as-formed solution was then transferred into an 80 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 120 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling naturally, the obtained sample was centrifuged and washed with DMF, methanol, and ethanol. The final product was dried under vacuum drying oven at 80 °C for 24 h.

The bare glass carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with 0.05 μM Al2O3 powder, and sonicated with ethanol and ultrapure water for 5 min. Then, the GCE was modified with 5 μL UiO-66-CNTs (0.1 mg mL−1). And UiO-66-CNTs/GCE was obtained after drying.

Three complementary chains (CSTri: CS1, CS2, and CS3) were treated with preparation buffer at 4 ℃ for 1 h. Followed by, 10 μL of the solution was dripped onto UiO-66-CNTs/GCE, and incubated at 4 ℃ with 100% humidity for 2 h. The -SH at the end of CSs was combined with the -NH2 in UiO-66-CNTs by electrostatic adsorption so that CSs were fixed on the surface of the electrode. After passivating the uncovered sites on the electrode surface through incubation with 10 μL MCH (1 mM) at 4 ℃ for 1 h, the CSTri/GCE was obtained. Similarly, CSBi/GCE was constructed using two complementary chains (CSBi: CS1 and CS3) with the same procedure.

The CSTri/GCE was further immersed with CDP-PBA and CDH-HBA in hybridization buffer. After incubation at 37 °C for 40 min and elution of the electrode surface with washing buffer, the M-shaped functional DNA complexes were formed and named M-shaped/GCE. Meanwhile, we prepared series of control electrodes by similar procedure. L-shaped/GCE was constructed with CSBi/GCE instead of CSTri/GCE. When CSTri/GCE reacted only with CDP-PBA or CDH-HBA, CDP-PBA/GCE or CDH-HBA/GCE was gained. M′-shaped/GCE was prepared by hybridizing CDP-NPA, CDH-NHA with CSTri/GCE, while L′-shaped/GCE was constructed by combining CDP-NPA, CDH-NHA, and CSBi/GCE. In addition, CSTri, CDP-PBA, and CDH-HBA were reacted in hybridization buffer and formed M-shaped functional DNA complexes, then the DNA complexes were fixed onto UiO-66-CNTs/GCE, and the obtained electrode was called M-shaped (solution)/GCE.

The above series of functional Apts/GCE were incubated with desired concentrations of Pb2+ or Hg2+ at RT and then washed to get Pb2+&Hg2+/GCE. The binding of targets to Apts led to reduced I CDP and I CDH.

In the three-electrode system, the series of functional Apts/GCE and the Pb2+&Hg2+/GCE were utilized as working electrodes. Ag/AgCl (sat.) and Pt wire were used as reference and counter-electrode, respectively. DPV signals were recorded for target quantification, in which the scanning potential ranged from 0 to 1.1 V and the pulse amplitude was 0.005 V. Three peak currents of the prepared functional Apts/GCE at 0.23 V, 0.55 V, and 0.84 V, could be attributed to CDP (I CDP), CDH (I CDP) and UiO-66-CNTs (I UiO-66-CNTs). The current ratios, I CDP/I UiO-66-CNTs, and I CDH/I UiO-66-CNTs were applied to quantify Pb2+ and Hg2+. Furthermore, we employed UiO-66-CNTs/GCE to directly analyze and detect Pb2+ and Hg2+ via ASV. Relevant conditions for ASV testing were detailed in the Supporting Information.

Eight species of aquatic edible vegetables, namely Oenanthe javanica, Brasenias chreberi, Eleocharis dulcis, Sagittaria trifolia, Watercaltrop, Euryales Semen, Lotus root and Wildrice stem, were collected from vegetable markets and supermarkets in Nanjing. Each species was randomly purchased 1 kg each time. After five random purchases, a total of forty samples were collected. The edible part of aquatic edible vegetables was washed with ultrapure water. Samples of the same species were crushed and mixed homogeneously and divided into five equal parts. Water and soil samples were collected from the cultivation area of aquatic edible vegetables. Each water sample was taken at 50 mL at a time, and each soil sample was taken at 500 g at a time. Five samples were collected for both water and soil samples after five random collections.

For aquatic edible vegetables and soil samples, 0.2 g of each sample was weighed in the digestion tank. With the addition of 5 mL nitric acid, digestion tank was placed overnight and then kept at 120 ℃ for 5 min, 150 ℃ for 10 min, and 20 min at 190 ℃ for digestion. After cooling, kept the digestion tank at 100 ℃ for 30 min, and filled it with ultrapure water to 25 mL. Besides, water samples were filtered to remove the impurities using 0.22 µm membranes. The samples were analyzed by ICP-MS and the established method, and each sample was measured five times in parallel.

Herein, UiO-66-CNTs have a large specific surface area, intrinsic and stable electrochemical signal, and were used to modify electrode and load CSs, also as an internal reference. Thus, micromorphology, compositional element, functional groups, and molecular structure of the prepared UiO-66-CNTs were characterized and analyzed by SEM, EDS, FTIR, and 1H NMR.

In addition, the specific surface areas of the two different modified electrodes were calculated using the Randles-Sevcik equation [32]. The calculated surface areas of bare GCE, UiO-66/GCE, and UiO-66-CNTs/GCE were 0.071, 0.220, and 0.305 cm2, respectively, indicating a 3.1-fold increase in surface area of UiO-66/GCE compared to bare GCE, due to the high porosity of UiO-66, which can effectively improve the specific surface area. After further doping MWCNTs with high surface area, the specific surface area of UiO-66-CNTs/GCE increased to 0.305 cm2, which was 1.4-fold higher than UiO-66/GCE and 4.3-fold higher than bare GCE. EIS (inset of Fig. 4) and specific surface area calculation demonstrated that the prepared UiO-66-CNTs had good conductivity and could effectively increase the specific surface area, so UiO-66-CNTs were preferred as the electrode modification material. Thus, SEM, EDS, FTIR, and 1H NMR demonstrated the successful synthesis of the UiO-66-CNTs. The rough surface, high specific surface area, and -NH2 group of UiO-66-CNTs make it an ideal carrier for loading CSs.

In this work, two CDs, CDP and CDH were synthesized as response signals and for labelling Apts and non-Apts. The micromorphology, optical, and electrical properties of prepared CDs were characterized and analyzed by DLS, SEM, fluorescence, UV-Vis, and DPV.

As depicted in the upper layer of Fig. 3A and B, CDP and CDH appeared brown and green under daylight (450 nm), respectively. While the bright yellow and blue fluorescence were observed under 365 nm UV light as shown in the low layer of Fig. 3A and B. Zeta-potential of CDP and CDH were shown in Fig. 3C, which were measured to be 57.1 mV and 65.9 mV. Positive potential of the CDs made it feasible to label negative charge chains by electrostatic attraction. The microscopic morphology was studied by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3D and E, CDP and CDH both showed a homogeneous spherical structure with the size of less than 10 nm. In addition, both CDs exhibited some degree of aggregation. This phenomenon maybe due to the high surfactant activity of CDs, which made them more likely to clump together to form larger agglomerates with several weak junctions. Some degree of aggregation was possibly responsible for strong electrochemical signals of CDs. The inset showed HRTEM images of CDs. CDP and CDH both exhibited well-resolved lattice stripes with the spacing of 0.29 nm and 0.26 nm, respectively, corresponding to the in-plane lattice of graphene (100)29. Hydrated particle size refers to the size of particles in a solution. The hydrated particle size of CDP and CDH was shown in Fig. 3F. The Hydrated particle size of CDs was obtained after dialyzing the samples with the HIMEDIA-110 dialysis membrane followed by filtration with a 0.22 µm cellulose nitrate membrane. As shown in Fig. 3F, the hydrated particle size of CDH ranged from 2 to 6 nm, with an average size of 5.04 nm. While that of CDP ranged from 3 to 8 nm, with an average size of 3.97 nm. These results were in agreement with the size of CDs observed in TEM images [26].

The UV–vis spectroscopy of CDs was depicted in Fig. 3G. It can be seen that the absorption peaks of CDP and CDH located at around 280 nm and 340 nm, which were frequently attributed to the π-π * transition of the aromatic CC and the n-π * transition of conjugated CO and CN. The fluorescence emission spectroscopy was shown in Fig. 3H. At the maximum excitation wavelengths of 330 nm and 360 nm, the optimal emission wavelengths were located at 570 nm and 460 nm, corresponding to CDP and CDH, respectively. In addition, the fluorescence and DPV signal intensity of CDs at a high-concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1 and a low-concentration of 0.02 mg mL−1 were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3H, compared with the signal at low-concentration, the fluorescence signal of CDs at high-concentration was significantly weakened. However, we surprisingly found that the DPV signal of CDs at high-concentration were significantly higher than those at low-concentration (Fig. 3I). From the comparison of Fig. 3H and I, it can be seen that the fluorescence signal of CDs at high-concentration tended to decrease through the self-quenching effect within a certain concentration range, while the DPV signal behaved in the opposite way, which was enhanced at high- concentration due to a certain degree of aggregation [33]. Meanwhile, distinct and stable oxidation peaks were observed at 0.23 V and 0.55 V in the DPV curves of CDs, which were related to the CDP and CDH (Fig. 3I). It might be attributed to the fact that the different carbon and nitrogen sources of CDP (citric acid/urea) and CDH (o-PD), and they may be formed via different pathways and mechanisms. Thus, the degree of N element doping and the number of oxygen-containing functional groups are different, which eventually led to the different DPV peak situation of CDP and CDH. Therefore, the DPV signal of CDs was chosen as the detection signal for subsequent experiments. Prior to the experiment, the concentrations of CDs with large and stable signals were screened by DPV experiments, which turned out to be 0.1 mg mL−1. This concentration of CDs was used to label aptamers for optimal detection limits.

The fabrication process of the ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor was studied with EIS using the Fe(CN)6 [3-/4-] as the redox probe. As shown in Fig. 4, the electron transfer resistance (Ret) of bare GCE was estimated to be 347 Ω (curve a). UiO-66/GCE and UiO-66-CNTs/GCE were prepared by equal concentration of solutions UiO-66 and UiO-66-CNTs and further tested the Ret. As shown in the inset in Fig. 4, the Ret of UiO-66/GCE was 642.8 Ω (curve c′), but the Ret of UiO-66-CNTs/GCE was reduced to 137 Ω (curve b′). It might be attributed to the fact that the good conductivity of MWCNTs. Thus, the application of the UiO-66-CNTs to modify GCE can facilitate the electron transfer on the electrode surface. When CSTri were attached to the UiO-66-CNTs/GCE, the Ret was increased to 672 Ω (curve c). Then, CDP-PBA and CDH-HBA were hybridized with CSTri. It was observed that the Ret continued to increase, and reached 1753 Ω (curve d). It was consistent with the fact that the layer of nucleic acid molecules can insulate the conductive support and perturb the interfacial electron transfer between the electrode and the electroactive species (Fe(CN)6 [3-/4-]) in solution. In the presence of 10 ng mL−1 Pb2+and 10 ng mL−1 Hg2+, the aptasensor showed a dramatically decreased Ret of 908 Ω, which could be explained by the cleavage of CDP-PBA and CDH-HBA from the Apt/GCE after the formation of G-quadruplex and T-Hg2+-T complexes [34,35]. Thus, these results revealed the successful construction of the aptasensor for simultaneous analysis of Pb2+ and Hg2+.

As a convenient electrochemical technique, DPV has the advantages of lower capacitive and background response, as well as higher current sensitivity. Therefore, DPV was adopted to compare the analytical performances of series functional Apts /GCE by recording their DPV response toward Hg2+ and Pb2+.

Considering the large intrinsic binding constants for complementary base pairing, the addition of the Apts may result in detachment of the CSs from the surface of the CSTri/GCE. To investigate whether the incorporation of Apts would have an effect on the detection performance, we prepared the M-shaped/GCE and M-shaped (solution)/GCE. As shown in the Fig S3, the DPV signals of CDs produced by both modified electrodes were essentially the same, indicating that the addition of Apts on the surface of the CSTri/GCE had little effect on the detection performance. As shown in Fig. 5A, B, and C, in the absence of the targets, the L′-shaped/GCE, M′-shaped/GCE, and L-shaped/GCE showed three large and well-defined peaks of I CDP centered at 0.23 V, I CDH at 0.55 V and I UiO-66-CNTs at 0.84 V, which arose from the oxidation of the corresponding redox probe of CDP, CDH and UiO-66-CNTs (curve a). In Fig. 5A and B, after addition of the Pb2+ and Hg2+ (curve b), no significant changes were showed in I CDP and I CDH of L′-shaped/GCE and M′-shaped/GCE compared to the absence of the targets. This could be explained by the non-Apts in M′-shaped/GCE and L′-shaped/GCE, which were disabled to recognize and bind specifically to targets, and thus it was not possible to cause signal changes. While significant signals reduction was observed after the incubation of targets with the M-shaped/GCE (curve c). Moreover, the comparison of the sensitivity of L-shaped/GCE and M-shaped/GCE to the targets was conducted as shown in Fig. 5C. With the addition of Pb2+ and Hg2+, the L-shaped/GCE (curve b) and M-shaped/GCE (curve c) showed a significant reduction of 11.3% and 30.6% in I CDP and of 15.3% and 27.2% in I CDH. The larger responses of ΔI CDP and ΔI CDH for M-shaped/GCE may be ascribed to the following factor: Compared with L-shaped structure, M-shaped DNA with a rigid structure allowed for the orderly fixation of the CDP-PBA and CDH-HPA on the surface of the CSTri/GCE, while the linear DNA was prone to tangling, tilt and elastic bending and thus affected signal generation and the specific binding between Apts and targets. In addition, the UiO-66-CNTs remained anchored on the GCE during the recognition process, and I UiO-66-CNTs was thus unchanged. These results indicated good feasibility of constructed aptasensor for simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+.

Further, the cross-reactivity of the series of functional Apts/GCE was further explored. The CDP-PBA/GCE or CDH-HBA/GCE were prepared after the assembly of CDP-PBA or CDH-HBA with CSTri/GCE. Therefore, CDP-PBA/GCE and CDH-HBA/GCE showed a DPV signal for only one CD as shown in Fig. 5D and E (curve a). With the presence of targets, the signals of CDs indicated a decreasing trend (curve b), suggesting that Apts only bound specifically to the corresponding target. In addition, we further added single or two targets to M-shaped/GCE to assess the cross-reactivity. As shown in Fig. 5F, the M-shaped/GCE exhibited three separate oxidation peaks at 0.23 V, 0.55 V, and 0.84 V in the blank detection buffer (curve a). After incubation of the M-shaped/GCE in 10 ng mL−1 Pb2+, the oxidation peak of the CDP-PBA was decreased significantly at 0.23 V, while the oxidation peaks of CDH-HBA and UiO-66-CNTs were unchanged at 0.55 V and 0.84 V (curve b). Likely, when M-shaped/GCE was further reacted with 10 ng mL−1 Hg2+, a reduction of the peak at 0.55 V was observed, but the peaks at 0.23 V and 0.84 V were not affected (curve c), which suggested that only the state of CDH-HBA was changed due to the formation of T-Hg2+-T structure via coordination. These results indicated that the proposed aptasensor had the ability to quantify either Pb2+ or Hg2+. More importantly, when M-shaped/GCE was further soaked into a mixed solution of 10 ng mL−1 Pb2+ and Hg2+, simultaneous decreases in the two peaks at 0.23 V and 0.55 V were found (curve d), while the peak of UiO-66-CNTs at 0.84 V remained constant. Moreover, we found that the peak currents at 0.23 V and 0.55 V showed a greater change as the concentration of Pb2+ and Hg2+ in the mixed solution increased to 100 ng mL−1 (curve e), which also implied that the system showed a good dose-signal correlation. The above results showed that M-shaped/GCE can achieve single target detection or dual targets simultaneous detection.

To achieve optimal current signals, the experimental conditions, including the concentration of UiO-66-CNTs, CSTri and Apts, hybridization temperature and time, targets incubation time, and pH of the detection buffer were optimized.

During the detection process, UiO-66-CNTs was used not only for loading CSTri but also as a reference signal. Its concentration would affect the detection performance. The concentration of UiO-66-CNTs was optimized by exploring the signal of the CDs at different concentrations. In Fig. S4A, the peak current of UiO-66-CNTs at 0.84 V increased with increasing concentration. And the peak currents of CDP and CDH at 0.23 V and 0.55 V were almost same at the UiO-66-CNTs concentrations of 0.5 and 1 mg mL−1 (curve a and b). When the UiO-66-CNTs concentration was too low, it produced a small reference signal, which may lead to a large error during the measurement process. When the UiO-66-CNTs concentrations were of 1.5 and 2 mg mL−1 (curve c and d), the signals of the CDs were slightly reduced. This may be due to the fact that when the reference signal of UiO-66-CNTs was too large, the resolution between adjacent peaks was decrease, thus affecting the accurate quantification. So, 1 mg mL−1 of UiO-66-CNTs was selected for the preparation of UiO-66-CNTs/GCE in the experiments.

The efficiency of the hybridization reaction is closely related to the concentration of CSTri and Apts, hybridization time and temperature. As shown in Fig. S4B, the curve that the current ratios, I CDs/I UiO-66-CNTs varied with the CCSTri/CApts from 400:400–1200:1200 with an increment of 200 ng mL−1 was like an approximate parabola and its peak was about at 800:800. This may be due to the tendency of the chains to tangle when the concentration was too high. So, the concentration of CSTri and Apts was determined to be 800 ng mL−1. Meanwhile, the I CDs/I UiO-66-CNTs were measured at different hybridization time from 10 min to 70 min ( Fig. 6A). The results showed that both signals reached stability after 40 min due to the completion of the hybridization reaction. The hybridization temperatures were also optimized through recording the I CDs/I UiO-66-CNTs within 20 ℃− 50 ℃, which was set to 37 ℃ finally. (Fig. 6B).

The incubation time between the targets and Apts was another important parameter for the aptasensor. Pb2+ could chelate with the oxygen atom of carbonyl group in the guanine of the PBA with a binding constant of 1.5 × 106 M−1, thus allowing the single-chain PBA to fold into a G-quadruplex structure and to be shed from the CSTri/GCE surface [36]. Likely, Hg2+ could undergo a proton substitution reaction with the N3 on the thymine (T) of the HPA and thus form T-Hg2+-T structure with a binding constant of nearly 1.0 × 106 M−1 [37]. As shown in Fig. 6C, the I CDs/I UiO-66-CNTs gradually decreased with increasing incubation time and stabilized after 40 min. This indicated that the Pb2+ and Hg2+ completely reacted with PBA and HPA to form the above special complexes and detach from the CSTri/GCE surface. So, 40 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time.

DPV signal intensity was strongly dependent on the pH of the detection buffer. In the experiment, the DPV signals of I CDs/I UiO-66-CNTs were measured in detection buffer of different pH (5.0–9.0). As pH increased, the signals became larger and then smaller, and the maximum signal intensities were obtained in the pH 7.0–7.5 (Fig. 6D). It was consistent with the fact that DNA chains are usually unwound in high pH environments and can become unstable and prone to breakage. Moreover, under acidic conditions, hydrogen ions interact with amino or phosphate groups on the bases, destroying the stability of hydrogen bonds, leading to unwinding and deformation or even degradation of DNA molecular structure [38]. Consequently, 10 mM PBS buffer with pH 7.4 was used in the experiment.
The characteristics of the ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor were evaluated in terms of selectivity, stability, reproducibility, and regenerability.

To investigate the selectivity of the constructed aptasensor, several potentially interfering substances that commonly coexist with two targets in actual samples were selected and added to the buffer containing 1000 ng mL−1 Pb2+ and Hg2+. The interfering substances were 1000 times (1 mg mL−1) non-target cations (Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, and Cd2+), and 1000 times (1 mg mL−1) non-target inorganics and organics (Cl-, NO3 -, NO2 -, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, HPO4 2-, NH4 +, glucose, citrate, urea, and EDTA). According to the formulae I = 1/2 ∑mi zi^2 (I: the ionic strength, ∑mi: the sum of the molar concentrations of all the ions, zi: the charge number of the ion), the ionic strength of Pb2+ or Hg2+ were calculated to be 9.7 × 10−6 and 1.0 × 10−5 mol kg−1, while that of single and mixed interfering ions were from 0.018 to 0.232 mol kg−1. The ionic strength of the interfering ions was much higher than that of the target ions. It can be seen in Fig. 7A and B that no obvious changes were observed in the I CDs/I UiO-66-CNTs of interfering substances compared to that of the blank, while the I CDs/I UiO-66-CNTs of Pb2+ or Hg2+ was more than ten times lower than that of the blank. This suggested that the above interfering ions even with high ionic strength had no effect on selectivity of the aptasensor. Moreover, the cross-sensitivity of the aptasensor was also studied by analyzing a mixture of Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+. The I CDs/I UiO-66-CNTs of the mixture was almost same as that of the pure Pb2+ or Hg2+ only. These results demonstrated that the constructed aptasensor had good selectivity for Pb2+ and Hg2+.

Furthermore, the long-term stability of UiO-66-CNTs/GCE and M-shaped/GCE was evaluated. After 35 days of storage in the RT, the I UiO-66-CNTs value only dropped to 90% of the initial value, demonstrating superior stability of the material on the electrode surface (Fig. 7C). This can be attributed to the stability of the UiO-66-CNTs and the strong hydrophobic forces between the UiO-66-CNTs and the GCE. The stability of the M-shaped/GCE was assessed as well. After it was stored at 4 ℃ for a period of time, 10 ng mL−1 Pb2+ and 10 ng mL−1 Hg2+ were added and reacted. As shown in Fig. 7D, I CDP/UiO-66-CNTs and I CDH/UiO-66-CNTs retained 90.6% and 91.3% of their initial DPV signal responses within 24 days, implying the excellent stability of the M-shaped/GCE.

The proposed aptasensor was used to evaluate the DPV signal responses at the same Pb2+ and Hg2+ concentration under the ideal experimental circumstances in order to investigate the repeatability. According to Fig. 7E, after seven repeated measurements, the intra-day repeatability of Pb2+ and Hg2+ was 96.3%− 104.9% and 96.4%− 103.9%, respectively, with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.15% and 2.93%. The daytime reproducibility of M-shaped/GCE for Pb2+ and Hg2+ was obtained by measurements over 7 consecutive days, and turned to be 96.3%− 104.9% and 96.4%− 103.9% with the RSD of 2.68% and 2.82%. It was confirmed that the reproducibility of the aptasensor was satisfactory.

The regenerability of Apts/GCE (M-shaped/GCE) was assessed. In Fig. 7F, after the incubation of M-shaped/GCE with Pb2+ and Hg2+, I CDP/UiO-66-CNTs and I CDH/UiO-66-CNTs showed a decreasing trend due to the bindings of Pb2+ and CDP-PBA, Hg2+ and CDH-HBA. Thus, the M-shaped functional DNA complexes was also disrupted due to partial shedding of the CDP-PBA and CDH-HBA. Reassembled the CDP-PBA and CDH-HBA, patched the M-shaped functional DNA complexes, and the signals returned to the initial value. Repeated experiments showed that M-shaped/GCE was able to determine Pb2+ and Hg2+ over five cycles, indicating the good regeneration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the aptasensor toward Pb2+ and Hg2+ was investigated by probing the dependence of the electrochemical response of the sensor on the concentrations of the two HMIs. Fig. 8A showed the DPV curves of the Apt/GCE after interaction with increasing amounts of Pb2+ and Hg2+ under the optimal conditions. Both I CDP and I CDH decreased with increasing Pb2+ and Hg2+ concentrations, while I UiO-66-CNTs was unchanged. When Pb2+ and Hg2+ were detected using single signal of I CDP and I CDH, the calibration plots were shown in Fig. 8C and D. Furthermore, the ratios of I CDP/I UiO-66-CNTs and I CDH/I UiO-66-CNTs were also employed to determine Pb2+ and Hg2+. The corresponding calibration plots were shown in Fig. 8B. For Pb2+, the linear regression equation was I CDP/I UiO-66-CNTs = 3.12–0.85 log (CPb 2+ /ng mL −1). The linear range was from 5 to 1000 ng mL−1 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 2.0 ng mL−1. For Hg2+, the linear regression equation was I CDH/I UiO-66-CNTs = 5.66–0.86 log (CHg 2+ /ng mL −1), and the linear range was 1–1000 ng mL−1 with a LOD of 0.5 ng mL−1. We know that Pb2+ and Hg2+ have distinguishable specific redox signals. For the purpose of comparison with the above sensors, we directly measured Pb2+ and Hg2+ simultaneously using UiO-66-CNTs/GCE. Fig. S5A depicted the ASV response of UiO-66-CNTs/GCE toward Pb2+ and Hg2+ at various concentrations. And the corresponding calibration plots were shown in Fig. S5B. The peak currents were proportional to Pb2+ and Hg2+ concentration in the range of 20–500 ng mL−1 and 20–300 ng mL−1, and the LOD was 7 ng mL−1. Obviously, ratiometric strategy had superior LODs and detection ranges than ASV method. Furthermore, for Pb2+ and Hg2+, quantification by I CDP/I UiO-66-CNTs and I CDH/I UiO-66-CNTs had larger linear relative coefficient than quantification by I CDP and I CDH, I Pb 2+ and I Hg 2+, thus revealing the good accuracy and reliability of the ratiometric aptasensor based on M-shaped functional DNA complexes. In addition, the common analytical parameters of other works on simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ were seriously compared ( Table 1). Through a simple experimental procedure, our work realized the simultaneous detection of two HMIs and the analytical parameters were comparable to those of relevant works. Furthermore, the use of Apts significantly improved the specificity of this method. This also implied that it was applied to samples of complex matrix with better anti-interference ability.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of the proposed aptasensor, the standard solution was added to food, water, and soil samples by a standard addition method, and recovery experiments were performed ( Table 2). Before recovery experiments, three samples of each kind were randomly selected, and the HMIs levels in those samples were determined by ICP-MS and set as blank values. The recoveries of Pb2+ were 94.89–101.70% and that of Hg2+ were 97.74–103.70%. Besides, RSD were below 5.36%. These results indicated the ratiometric aptasensor application potential in actual samples determination.

To evaluate the applicability of this aptasensor, we compared the results with those obtained by ICP-MS. As illustrated in Table 3, the p-values were all greater than 0.05, indicating good agreement between this method and the standard method. Furthermore, the Pb2+ was detected in water, soil, and eight aquatic edible vegetables samples but the levels did not exceed the limits according to GB 2762–2017, GB 15618–2018, and GB 3838–2002. Among eight kinds of aquatic vegetable samples, the content of Pb2+ in Wildrice stem was relatively high, which probably could be explained by the strong capacity of epidermis of Wildrice stem for Pb2+ absorption and accumulation. In addition, Hg2+ was not detected in the water. Although it was detected in other samples but at very low levels, none of which exceeded the limits. In conclusion, the Pb2+ and Hg2+ in all samples were within the permissible limits, indicating that the safety of eight aquatic edible vegetables was better in Eastern China and these foods were safe for consumption. However, considering that Pb2+ and Hg2+ were detected in most samples, monitoring of food contamination and the growing environment should be further strengthened.

Herein, a ratiometric electrochemical aptasensor was developed for the simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+. The DPV signals of the CDs and UiO-66-CNTs were utilized as the response and the reference signals to achieve ratiometric detection. The DPV signals of the CDs was more stable and stronger than the commonly used fluorescence signals, and its own aggregation would further enhance the signals. In particular, the hybridization of two specific Apts with three CSs to form the rigid M-shaped DNA complexes evidently improved the stability of the proposed aptasensor. And M-shaped structure together with UiO-66-CNTs with high specific surface area contributed to enhance sensitivity. By integrating the specific recognition of Apts toward targets with the anti-interference property of the ratiometric strategy, the proposed method exhibited reasonable selectivity, operational convenience, excellent sensitivity and specificity for Pb2+ and Hg2+, with LOD of 2.0 and 0.5 ng mL−1. In terms of practical applications, the aptasensor was applied to systematically monitor Pb2+ and Hg2+ contamination levels in eight aquatic edible vegetables and their root water and soil. And the proposed aptasensor brought comparable results to those from ICP-MS. It is also a universal strategy of ratiometric aptasensor for the detection of HMIs in real samples, which holds great significance to environmental pollutants and risk assessment. We know that the Apt configuration can be changed flexibly, and the binding of the target to the Apt is a reversible reaction. However, the impact of reaction reversibility on the detection has not been studied and explored. In addition, the aptasensor can only achieve Pb2+ and Hg2+ detection in the ionic state. Metal organic compounds, such as methylmercury, could not yet be quantified.

Pb2+ and Hg2+ are highly bioavailable and toxic, causing neurological disorders, genotoxicity, cancer, and even death. And the two have synergistic effect, leading to greater toxicity when coexisting. Direct detection based on the DPV signal of HMIs is challenging due to trace presence in the environment and interferences from coexisting substances. In this work, we utilize Apts to improve specificity, ratiometric strategy to enhance interference immunity, special DNA structure, and MOF material to amplify signals, realizing accurate and simultaneous quantification of two trace substances. With promising application prospects, the method could be extended to other environmental pollutants and risk assessment.

Xiaoying Wang: Supervision, Methodology, Writing − review & editing. Mingming Xu: Investigation, Data curation, Writing − original draft. Yijing Kuang: Software, Visualization. Xiangping Liu: Resources, Formal analysis. Jinhua Yuan: Investigation.
